name: BTS World Tour 2026 — Busan Asiad Main Stadium
address: Asiad-daero, Yeonje-gu, Busan
description: The centerpiece event: BTS World Tour Busan leg at Asiad Main Stadium. Capacity 50,000+. This is your trip anchor — all pre/post activities revolve around this date.
whyItMatters: This is the anchor of your entire Busan trip. Every spot in this cluster is designed to maximize your experience before and after the show.
notice: Tickets sold via Weverse Shop. Foreign card accepted. Arrive 2 hours early for entry.

transitFromAnchor.description: 12 min by subway (Busan Metro Line 2) from Asiad Main Stadium Station

name: ZM-ILLENNIAL (지밀레니얼) — Daeyeon-dong ARMY Pilgrimage
address: Daeyeon-dong, Nam-gu, Busan (대연동, 남구, 부산)
description: ZM-ILLENNIAL (지밀레니얼) is the iconic ARMY fan spot near Daeyeon station in Busan's Nam-gu — a stone's throw from JIMIN's childhood neighborhood. Browse limited fan merch, join the pre-concert buzz with global ARMY, and leave a message on the fan tribute wall.
whyItMatters: This is where the ARMY pilgrimage begins. Every international JIMIN fan comes here first. Visit 1–2 days before the concert for maximum atmosphere and the best merch selection.
notice: Verify current exact address via BTS ARMY fan boards before visiting.

transitFromAnchor.description: 9 min by taxi or subway from Asiad Main Stadium

name: BTS Official Merch Pop-Up — BIFF Square, Nampo
address: BIFF Square, Nampo-dong, Jung-gu, Busan
description: Official BTS concert merchandise pop-up stores typically set up in BIFF Square and surrounding Nampo area 3–5 days before the concert. Get your official MD before it sells out — stock is always limited for foreign fans.
whyItMatters: Official concert MD sells out within hours. Arrive in Busan 2–3 days early and secure your goods.
notice: Lines form 2–3 hours before opening. Bring your concert ticket QR code for official MD verification.

transitFromAnchor.description: 20 min by taxi from Asiad Main Stadium. No direct subway — take taxi or bus.

name: Run BTS! Filming Trail — Gamcheon Culture Village
address: Gamcheon 2-dong, Saha-gu, Busan
description: Do this 1–2 days before the concert while Busan is still calm. Walk the exact alleys featured in 'Run BTS!'. Pick up the ₩2,000 trail map at the entrance — it marks every filming spot.
whyItMatters: Early-bird fans have the rare chance to explore Gamcheon without the massive concert-week crowds.
notice: ₩2,000 trail map at entrance. Best on weekday mornings. Steep hills — wear comfortable shoes.

name: Concert Day Survival Kit — Luggage Storage & Fast Routes
address: Asiad Station (Metro Line 3), Yeonje-gu, Busan
description: On concert day: store luggage at Busan Station coin lockers (₩3,000–6,000/day), take Metro Line 3 to Asiadaero Station. Exit 2 leads straight to the stadium north gate. Recommended arrival: 2.5 hours before showtime.
whyItMatters: Thousands of foreign fans get stuck on concert day without a plan. This entry gives you the exact logistics playbook.
notice: Coin lockers fill by noon on concert day. Arrive before 10 AM to secure storage.

transitFromAnchor.description: 14 min by subway (Line 2) from Asiad Main Stadium to Gwangalli Beach

name: Gwangalli Post-Show Night Gathering
address: Gwangalli 1-dong, Suyeong-gu, Busan
description: After the concert, ARMY fans flood Gwangalli Beach. The illuminated Gwangan Bridge reflects off the sea. Grab a cold beer from a convenience store, sit on the beach, and relive the show.
whyItMatters: The real party happens here after the show. Gwangalli is 14 minutes from the stadium.
notice: Free. Bring a light jacket. GS25 and CU convenience stores along the beach.

transitFromAnchor.description: 20 min by subway from Asiad Main Stadium to Haeundae Station (Line 2)

name: Haeundae Sunrise & Recovery Walk
address: Haeundae-gu, Busan
description: The day after the concert. Walk Haeundae Beach at sunrise (~6 AM in October), grab Korean-style hangover soup at a nearby 24-hour restaurant, and let the ocean do the healing.
whyItMatters: The morning after is magical. Haeundae at sunrise post-BTS concert is a bucket-list moment of its own.
notice: October sunrise in Busan ~06:20. 24-hour haejang-guk restaurants near Haeundae Station.

name: HYBE Insight — Official BTS Museum
address: 20 Hangang-daero, Yongsan-gu, Seoul
description: The official BTS & HYBE Artists museum. Immersive exhibitions, rare behind-the-scenes content, exclusive on-site merchandise. Advance reservation is mandatory.
whyItMatters: The single most important BTS pilgrimage site in Seoul. Non-negotiable for any ARMY visiting Korea.
notice: Advance reservation required. Book 2 weeks ahead. Closed Mondays.

name: BTS Love Lock Spot — N Seoul Tower
address: 105 Namsangongwon-gil, Yongsan-gu, Seoul
description: The fence around N Seoul Tower holds thousands of padlocks left by BTS members and ARMY fans from every country. Add your own lock, find the ones left by members. Best at sunset.
whyItMatters: A pilgrimage site, a photo spot, and a connection to BTS history — all in one.
notice: Cable car ₩9,500 round trip. Hiking trail free — ~25 min walk.

name: Hongdae K-Pop Street Live — Weekend Performances
address: Hongik University St., Mapo-gu, Seoul
description: Every Friday and Saturday evening, Hongdae becomes a massive open-air K-pop stage. Future idols, dance crews, and indie bands perform for free. This is K-pop before the stadium.
whyItMatters: Free K-pop at its most authentic — no tickets, no barriers, raw talent.
notice: Best Friday & Saturday 8–11 PM. Weather-dependent. Busiest near Exit 9.

name: Busan International Fireworks Festival 2026
address: Gwangalli Beach, Suyeong-gu, Busan
description: Korea's most spectacular fireworks festival over Gwangan Bridge. 1M+ visitors. Fireworks synchronized with music, lighting both sky and sea simultaneously.
whyItMatters: Once-a-year spectacle. If your trip dates overlap with late October in Busan, do not miss it.
notice: Free event. Arrive 3–4 hours early. Store all luggage beforehand.

name: Korea Stay Sale Festa 2026 — Up to 60% Off Hotels
address: Participating hotels nationwide (Seoul, Busan, Jeju, Gyeongju)
description: South Korea's government-backed hotel discount campaign. Partnered hotels offer up to 60% off for September. 4-star Seoul hotels can drop under $60/night.
whyItMatters: Best time of year to book Korean hotels. Trip can flex to September to save $200+.
notice: Book via KNTO official site or partnered OTAs. Limited rooms.

name: Taejongdae Resort Park
address: 179 Jeonmang-ro, Yeongdo-gu, Busan — Bus 8 or 30 from Nampo-dong (45 min)
description: Taejongdae is Busan's most dramatic natural park: sheer 60m sea cliffs, a historic lighthouse, and dense pine forest on Yeongdo Island. The Danubi mini-train (₩3,000, runs hourly) circles the 4km loop if you prefer not to walk. The observation deck at Youngdo Lighthouse offers sweeping views of the Tsushima islands on clear days. Entry ₩2,000 for foreign adults. Allotment: 2–3 hours.
whyItMatters: The raw, untouched coastline here is unlike anything in central Busan — sheer cliffs meeting the open sea, with zero crowds on weekday mornings.
notice: Danubi train runs every 30 min (₩3,000). Wear non-slip shoes on cliff paths. No subway access — taxi ₩12,000 from Nampo.

name: Songdo Aerial Cable Car
address: 171 Songdo Haean-ro, Seo-gu, Busan — Bus 7 or 71 from Nampo-dong (25 min)
description: Korea's first ocean aerial cable car reconnected in 2017, crossing 1.62km above the waters of Songdo Beach. The crystal-bottom gondola offers a stomach-dropping view straight down to the sea. Opens 09:00–22:00 daily. Round-trip tickets: ₩15,000 adults (regular cabin), ₩20,000 (crystal cabin with glass floor). The departure station is a short walk from Songdo Beach promenade.
whyItMatters: The only place in Busan to fly above open ocean in a cable car — the glass-floor cabin view is genuinely spectacular.
notice: Crystal cabin (glass floor) is ₩5,000 more. Book at the counter on weekdays, expect 20-min queues on weekends.

name: Busan Tower & Yongdusan Park
address: 120 Yongdusan-gil, Jung-gu, Busan — Nampo Station (Line 1, Exit 7) + escalator
description: Busan Tower (부산타워) stands 120m tall at the top of Yongdusan Park in the historic Jung-gu district. The observation deck offers a 360° panorama over Busan Port, the city skyline, and Yeongdo Island. Admission ₩12,000 for adults (foreign card OK). The park surrounding the tower is free — wide promenades, rose gardens, and an old-fashioned carousel. Reach via the covered escalator from Nampo Station Exit 7.
whyItMatters: The most accessible city panorama in Busan — and the park below is a peaceful escape from the shopping crowds of Nampo-dong.
notice: Tower admission ₩12,000. Park is free. Best at dusk when port lights up. Last elevator up 21:30.

name: Haedong Yonggungsa Temple
address: 86 Yonggung-gil, Gijang-gun, Busan — Bus 181 or 100 from Haeundae (40 min)
description: Unlike any temple you've seen in Korea — Haedong Yonggungsa sits directly on a jagged coastal cliff, with waves crashing below the prayer halls. Built in 1376, expanded in 1930. The approach passes through a 108-step staircase, a dragon statue, and 12 zodiac animal sculptures. Entry is free. Best visited at sunrise or on full-moon nights when Buddhist rites are held. Foreign cards accepted at the small food stalls near the entrance.
whyItMatters: The sea-cliff setting makes this the most visually dramatic temple in all of Busan — arguably in all of Korea. Nothing else looks like this.
notice: Free entry. 108 steps to the main gate — not wheelchair accessible. Bus 181 from Haeundae (40 min). Cash only at street stalls.

name: Shinsegae Centum City & Spa Land
address: 35 Centumdong-ro, Haeundae-gu, Busan — Centum City Station (Line 2, Exit 12)
description: The world's largest department store (Guinness World Record). Shinsegae Centum City houses Spa Land on the 7th floor — a premium Korean jjimjilbang with outdoor pools, 13 themed sauna rooms, and a Bulgari-designed interior. Entry ₩18,000 weekdays / ₩22,000 weekends. The department store below has a full tax-refund desk on B1 (bring passport). Ice rink, cinema multiplex, and food hall all inside. Foreign cards accepted everywhere.
whyItMatters: Where locals come on rainy days: world-class spa + Korea's most impressive mall under one roof. The outdoor rooftop pool with city views is unmissable.
notice: Spa Land towel rental included. Swimwear required for pool areas. Tax refund desk on B1 — min purchase ₩30,000. Bring passport.

name: Seomyeon Food Street (서면 먹자골목)
address: Seomyeon-ro, Busanjin-gu, Busan — Seomyeon Station (Line 1/2, Exit 1)
description: Busan's ultimate late-night food destination. Dozens of restaurants line the narrow alleys of Seomyeon: dakgalbi (spicy stir-fried chicken, ₩12,000/person), sundae gukbap (blood sausage soup), tteokbokki, and fried chicken towers. Seomyeon is also the place for the famous 'makgeolli tent bars' (machaengi pojangmacha) — canvas-covered tents selling rice wine and snacks. Open until 02:00–03:00. Foreign cards accepted at most restaurants.
whyItMatters: Where Busan's young generation eats and drinks at night. Skip the tourist-facing restaurants — walk down the alleys to find the real experience.
notice: Budget ₩15,000–₩25,000/person. Point and order — picture menus common. Busiest 19:00–23:00.

name: Gukje Market (국제시장)
address: Gukje-sijang 1-gil, Jung-gu, Busan — Nampo Station (Line 1, Exit 3), 5-min walk
description: Korea's largest and oldest traditional market, originally formed by Korean War refugees in the 1950s. Today it spans 6 sections: Section 1 has clothing and fabric; Section 2 has imported goods; Sections 3–6 have electronics, toys, and kitchenware. Street food throughout: hotteok (sweet pancake, ₩1,500), bibimbap ₩7,000. Cash preferred. Open 09:00–20:00 (most stalls). Closed on Sundays in some sections.
whyItMatters: The historical heart of Busan's commerce — and the same market featured in the Korean film 'Ode to My Father'. Zero tourist-trap pricing.
notice: Cash only at most stalls. Section 2 open Sundays. ATM at Nampo Station exit. Haggling acceptable on larger items.

name: Dongbaek Island & APEC House
address: Woo-dong, Haeundae-gu, Busan — Dongbaek Station (Line 2, Exit 1) or walk from Haeundae Beach
description: A small forested peninsula extending into the sea at the west end of Haeundae Beach. The 1km scenic loop trails through camellia forest (동백꽃, blooms Nov–Mar), sea-view overlooks, and passes APEC House — the conference center used for the 2005 APEC Summit. Free entry. The 'Oryukdo' multi-star views of Gwangan Bridge from the tip of the island are outstanding at night. Easy 30-minute loop walk at a relaxed pace.
whyItMatters: A quiet green pocket 5 minutes from crowded Haeundae Beach — camellias in winter, ocean views all year, zero entry fee.
notice: Free. Walk from Haeundae Beach (10 min) or Dongbaek Station. Camellia blooms November–March.

name: UN Memorial Cemetery
address: 93 Liancourt-ro, Nam-gu, Busan — Bus 68 or 131 to UN Memorial Cemetery stop
description: The world's only United Nations-managed cemetery — the final resting place of 2,300 soldiers from 11 countries who died during the Korean War (1950–53). Immaculately maintained. The visitor center has English audio guides (free) and a moving digital tribute wall. Changing of the guard ceremony on weekdays at 10:00 and 14:00. Smart, respectful dress is appreciated. Free entry. Open daily except Mondays (October–March).
whyItMatters: An unexpectedly powerful and moving experience in the middle of a modern city — the scale of sacrifice becomes real here in a way that no museum can replicate.
notice: Free entry. Closed Mondays Oct–Mar. Guard ceremony 10:00 & 14:00 weekdays. Avoid sleeveless tops or shorts as a mark of respect.

name: Oryukdo Skywalk
address: 170 Oryukdo-ro, Nam-gu, Busan — Bus 24 or 131 to Oryukdo Skywalk stop
description: A 15m glass-floor observation deck cantilevered over a 35m sea cliff on the southern tip of Busan. Look straight down through the floor to the ocean and rocks below. Shoe covers are provided at the entrance (mandatory). Free entry. The walk from the parking lot to the skywalk is 500m of scenic coastal path. Connects to the start of the Igidae Coastal Walk for those wanting a full-day coastal hike. Open 09:00–18:00 daily.
whyItMatters: Five seconds on the glass floor over a 35m cliff tells you more about Busan's raw natural power than an hour of beach walking.
notice: Free. Shoe covers provided (mandatory). No subway — Bus 24 or 131 from Gwangan or Namcheon Station.

name: Dalmaji Hill Café Street
address: Dalmaji-gil, Haeundae-gu, Busan — 20-min walk from Haeundae Beach (east end)
description: Dalmaji Hill (달맞이길) is a winding cliff-top road above the sea between Haeundae and Songjeong beaches. Both sides of the road are lined with independent cafés, each competing for the best ocean view. Seating starts at ₩8,000–₩15,000 for a coffee with a panoramic sea terrace. Most cafés open from 11:00–22:00. On clear days you can see from Gwangan Bridge all the way to Haedong Temple in the distance. This is Busan's answer to Santorini.
whyItMatters: Busan's most romantic stretch of road — every turn reveals a sea-view terrace. The crowd here is different from the beach: quieter, more local.
notice: No parking on weekends. Walk or take bus from Haeundae. Best at golden hour (16:00–18:00). Budget ₩10,000–₩15,000 for coffee.

name: Millak Raw Fish Center
address: Millak-ro, Suyeong-gu, Busan — Walk 15 min from Gwangan Station (Line 2, Exit 5)
description: Millak Raw Fish Center (민락회타운) is a 5-story building on Suyeong Bay where 40+ seafood restaurants operate side by side. Buy your raw fish (hoe/회) directly from the ground-floor vendor tanks, then take it upstairs to any restaurant for preparation (cooking fee ₩5,000–₩10,000). The upstairs restaurants face the bay with Gwangan Bridge framing every view. Open from morning until late night. Cash preferred; some restaurants now accept cards.
whyItMatters: Real Busan raw fish culture — locals argue Millak fish is fresher than Jagalchi because the vendors are smaller and turn over faster.
notice: Cash strongly preferred. Buy fish on ground floor (₩20,000–₩40,000) → take to upstairs restaurant for cooking (₩5,000 cooking fee). Point to order.

name: Busan Cinema Center (BIFF)
address: 120 Suyeong-ro, Haeundae-gu, Busan — Centum City Station (Line 2, Exit 7)
description: The permanent home of the Busan International Film Festival (BIFF), opened 2011. The iconic 163m cantilevered roof — the world's longest single-span LED roof — covers an outdoor plaza that hosts BIFF screenings every October. Outside festival season, the Cinema Center runs daily screenings of Korean and international films (tickets ₩12,000–₩15,000). The LED roof illumination show runs nightly at 19:30 and 21:00 for 10 minutes — completely free to watch from outside.
whyItMatters: The nightly LED light show on the world's longest cantilevered roof is free and genuinely spectacular — a piece of architectural performance art in the middle of Centum City.
notice: LED roof show: 19:30 & 21:00 daily (free from outside). Film tickets ₩12,000–₩15,000. Best seat for light show: the steps across from the main entrance.

name: Choryang Ibagu-gil 168 Steps
address: Ibagu-gil, Choryang-dong, Dong-gu, Busan — Busan Station (Exit 7), 10-min walk
description: Ibagu-gil (이바구길) is a hillside alley trail in the Choryang-dong neighborhood above Busan Station, developed by residents displaced by the Korean War. The highlight is the 168-step staircase (168 계단) — you can ride the free monorail (₩500, 2 min) instead if stairs aren't your thing. At the top: a sweeping view over Busan port and the Old Downtown. Street art, traditional houses, and grandmothers selling homemade snacks line the 1km route. Free to explore.
whyItMatters: The last surviving old neighborhood of Busan's downtown — layers of war-era history in a 15-minute walk from the train station. Almost no tourists.
notice: Monorail ₩500 (avoids 168 steps). Free to explore. Snacks are cash-only. Busiest on weekends; quieter weekday afternoons.

name: SEA LIFE Busan Aquarium
address: 266 Haeundaehaebyeon-ro, Haeundae-gu, Busan — Haeundae Station (Line 2, Exit 5)
description: Opened in 1998, Korea's first and largest marine aquarium — 250 species, 35,000 sea creatures, and a 80m glass-tunnel shark tank. The highlight is the 4-minute walk through the underwater tunnel as sharks and rays glide overhead. Tickets ₩27,000 adults (foreign card OK; 10% discount on Klook). Open daily 10:00–22:00. Closes 21:00 last entry. Directly on Haeundae Beach — combine with a beach day.
whyItMatters: Sharks swimming overhead while standing on a glass floor on the Haeundae beachfront — hard to beat for a morning activity before the beach crowd arrives.
notice: Tickets ₩27,000 adults — 10% cheaper via Klook. Last entry 21:00. The shark tunnel is busiest 13:00–16:00; go at 10:00 for shortest queues.
